title: `HEALTHCHECK` `--start-interval` parameter produces "not defined" error
body:
Issue [#21701](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21701) reported a problem where the `HEALTHCHECK` `--start-interval` parameter was producing an error.

PR #5472 added support for `--start-interval` and closed [#21701](https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/21701).

However, as of today (2025-03-03), a Dockerfile containing:

``` Dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=8s --retries=2 --start-period=60s --start-interval=3s \
  CMD ["/bin/sh", "/usr/sbin/healthcheck.sh"]
```

is producing this error:

```
[2/2] STEP 9/9: HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=8s --retries=2 --start-period=60s --start-interval=3s   CMD ["/bin/sh", "/usr/sbin/healthcheck.sh"]
flag provided but not defined: -start-interval
Usage:
  -interval string
    	
  -retries string
    	
  -start-period string
    	
  -timeout string
    	
Error: building at STEP "HEALTHCHECK --interval=60s --timeout=8s --retries=2 --start-period=60s --start-interval=3s CMD /bin/sh /usr/sbin/healthcheck.sh": flag provided but not defined: -start-interval
```

Using either `docker build` or `docker buildx build` with the same Dockerfile does not produce this error.

At [github.com/zyclonite/zerotier-docker PR38](https://github.com/zyclonite/zerotier-docker/pull/38), this issue appears to be behind all these failures:

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/285529f5-34b1-414b-acd8-d4550f7d67cb)

Has the PR #5472 fix fallen out?

For the record, this is from my local build system (where I can replicate the GitHub problem):

```
$ docker version
Client: Docker Engine - Community
 Version:           28.0.1
 API version:       1.48
 Go version:        go1.23.6
 Git commit:        068a01e
 Built:             Wed Feb 26 10:41:16 2025
 OS/Arch:           linux/amd64
 Context:           default

Server: Docker Engine - Community
 Engine:
  Version:          28.0.1
  API version:      1.48 (minimum version 1.24)
  Go version:       go1.23.6
  Git commit:       bbd0a17
  Built:            Wed Feb 26 10:41:16 2025
  OS/Arch:          linux/amd64
  Experimental:     false
 containerd:
  Version:          1.7.25
  GitCommit:        bcc810d6b9066471b0b6fa75f557a15a1cbf31bb
 runc:
  Version:          1.2.4
  GitCommit:        v1.2.4-0-g6c52b3f
 docker-init:
  Version:          0.19.0
  GitCommit:        de40ad0

$ buildah --version
buildah version 1.28.2 (image-spec 1.1.0-rc2, runtime-spec 1.0.2-dev)

$ uname -a
Linux prx-deb 6.1.0-30-amd64 #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Debian 6.1.124-1 (2025-01-12) x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

I do not know which version of `buildah` is being used on GitHub.

comments.body:
Hi @Paraphraser , Your buildah version looks relatively old, the PR you have tagged got added to buildah in `v1.37.0` I think. I would recommend moving to newer versions of podman/buildah and test this.

Thanks

Hi @flouthoc - thanks for your reply.

According to [buildah install.md](https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/install.md):

* for Debian I need Bookworm plus a standard `apt install buildah`.
* for Ubuntu I need 20.10 or later, also with a standard `apt install buildah`.

I have:

* Debian Bookworm 12.9. The `apt` install gets me `buildah version 1.28.2`.
* Ubuntu 24.04.2 Noble Numbat. The `apt` install gets me `buildah version 1.33.7`.

Both of those are less than the v1.37.0 you mention and, as expected, both produce the same error about `--start-interval`.

You recommend that I move to newer versions.

I would be happy to do that but when I follow the [buildah install.md](https://github.com/containers/buildah/blob/main/install.md) instructions, I get obsolete versions.

All other things being equal, doesn't that mean either:

1. More recent versions of buildah aren't actually making it to the `apt` backing stores; or
2. Something about my Debian and Ubuntu systems is preventing `apt` from fetching the latest and greatest version of buildah

Do you have any thoughts on how can I fix that?

<hr>

As a separate but related issue, the build failures occurring on GitHub would seem to have the same cause. I don't know how to confirm the version of buildah being used for those automated builds but it seems likely that whatever is stopping `apt` from getting a more-recent release on my own systems is also affecting the mechanism(s) by which GitHub comes to use buildah.

So, in that sense, isn't the error about `--start-interval` really telling us there's a serious supply-chain problem somewhere? Do you want to re-open this issue and rename it to something like `apt repositories have obsolete releases of buildah`, or should I open a new issue with that title?
